t: AI FUTURES
TRAINING PROGRAM
A 2-Week Intensive for Young Innovators (Age 12)

rows:
📋  FACILITATOR EDITION  📋
Weeks 1–2  |  10 Days  |  2.5 Hours Per Day

t: Programme Overview

The AI Futures Training Program is a 10-day intensive designed to give 12-year-olds the core knowledge, practical skills, and ethical frameworks they need to thrive in a world shaped by artificial intelligence.

This is not a course about learning to code (though we do build things). It is not a course about ChatGPT hacks. It is a course about thinking — about understanding AI at a level deep enough that you can evaluate it critically, use it creatively, and shape it responsibly.
Program Structure

rows: Days 1–5. Core knowledge: what AI is, how it learns, how to direct it, how it perceives the world, and where it fails. Heavy on concepts, experiments, and critical thinking.
Days 6–10. Applied learning: AI as a creative partner, design thinking with AI, and a 3-day build sprint culminating in Demo Day — a live showcase for parents and guests.

t: What Students Will Walk Away With
A clear, accurate mental model of what AI is and how it works
Practical prompt engineering skills they can use immediately
A completed AI project they designed and built themselves
A personal ethical framework for evaluating AI systems
The vocabulary to participate in AI conversations at school, home, and eventually work
A "Letter to Future Self" — a personal commitment to their AI learning journey

Read the "Key Concepts" sections at your own pace — they are written for you, not for a university lecture.
Complete every reflection exercise. The thinking you do in your journal is more valuable than any activity.

Tool 1 — Claude (Anthropic)

rows:
Claude is an AI assistant made by Anthropic. It understands and generates human language — you talk to it in plain English (or Chinese), and it responds intelligently. Claude powers this very training program.
Access: claude.ai (web browser, no install needed)
Free tier: Available — suitable for all program activities
Used in: Days 3, 6, 7, 8, 9 (prompt engineering, creative work, project building)

t: How to Use Claude in This Program
Go to claude.ai and create a free account using your email
Start a new conversation. Type your message in the input box at the bottom.
Press Enter or click the send button. Claude will respond immediately.
You can continue the conversation — Claude remembers what was said earlier in the same chat.
To start fresh (e.g. for a new activity), click "New Conversation" in the sidebar.

rows:
Always start with context: tell Claude who you are and what you're working on
Be specific: vague questions get vague answers
Iterate: if the first response isn't right, ask Claude to refine it
Verify: never accept factual claims without checking from another source
Copy useful responses into your own document — Claude conversations are temporary

t: Tool 2 — Claude Code

rows:
Claude Code is Anthropic's AI coding assistant. Unlike the web chat, Claude Code works alongside your code editor — it can read your files, write code, fix bugs, and explain concepts in the context of your actual project.
Access: Via terminal (claude command) or VS Code extension
Free tier: Available with Anthropic account
Used in: Days 8, 9 (project build days — optional for advanced students)

t: Getting Started with Claude Code

rows:
Option A — Web-based (easiest for beginners):
No install needed. Use claude.ai and paste your code into the chat.
Ask: "Here is my HTML file [paste code]. Can you add a button that shows the result?"
Option B — VS Code Extension (recommended for Track B projects):
Download VS Code from code.visualstudio.com (free)
Open VS Code → Click Extensions icon (left sidebar) → Search "Claude"
Install the official Anthropic Claude extension
Sign in with your Anthropic account
Highlight any code and press Cmd+Shift+P → type "Ask Claude"
📝  What Claude Code Can Do For Your Project

Write new code from a plain-English description: "Write a JavaScript function that takes a list of numbers and returns the average"
Explain code you don't understand: "Explain what this function does, line by line"
Find and fix bugs: "This code should sort alphabetically but it's not working. What's wrong?"
Suggest improvements: "How could I make this more efficient?"
Generate HTML/CSS layouts: "Create a simple webpage with a title, an image, and two buttons"
Help with your Teachable Machine export code (Track B)
⚠️  Important: Claude Code is a Collaborator, Not a Shortcut

The goal is NOT to have Claude Code write your entire project. The goal is to use it as a skilled collaborator — you make the creative and design decisions, Claude Code helps you implement them.
Always read and understand any code Claude Code produces before using it. If you don't understand it, ask Claude Code to explain it. Understanding > speed.

DAY 1
AI Is Already Around You
Demystifying the most talked-about technology of our time

t: Figure 1.1 — The AI Family Tree: from AI to Generative AI
Overview

Before we learn to build with Artificial Intelligence, we need to understand what it actually is — not the sci-fi robot version, but the real thing that is already shaping everything from what videos appear on your screen to how your phone unlocks with your face.

Today we pull back the curtain. By the end of this session, you will be able to confidently explain what AI is, where it already appears in your daily life, and what makes it different from regular computer programs.

rows:
Define Artificial Intelligence in plain language
Distinguish between AI, Machine Learning, and Deep Learning
Identify at least 10 examples of AI in everyday life
Understand the difference between Narrow AI and General AI
Bust common myths about what AI can and cannot do

t: Facilitator Schedule

rows: Opening discussion: "What do you think AI is?"

t: Key Concepts
1. What Is Artificial Intelligence?

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is the field of computer science that creates systems capable of performing tasks that normally require human intelligence — things like recognising images, understanding language, making decisions, and solving problems.
A simple way to think about it: a regular computer program does exactly what you tell it, step by step, every time. An AI system learns from examples and figures out its own rules.
2. The AI Family Tree
AI is a broad term. Here are the key levels within it:
Artificial Intelligence (AI): The big umbrella — any machine that mimics human thinking
Machine Learning (ML): A type of AI where the system learns from data without being explicitly programmed for each task
Deep Learning: A powerful type of ML that uses networks inspired by the human brain (called neural networks) — this is what powers image recognition and ChatGPT
Generative AI: AI that can create new content — text, images, music, code — based on what it has learned
3. Narrow AI vs. General AI
This is one of the most important distinctions to understand:

rows: General AI (science fiction, not real yet)
Can do any intellectual task a human can
Would learn, reason, and adapt like a person
Examples: Siri, Netflix recommendations, spam filters
Does not exist yet — still research only

t: 4. How Does AI Actually Learn?
The most common type of AI today learns through a process called Supervised Learning:
You give the AI thousands of examples with correct answers (e.g., 50,000 photos labelled "cat" or "not cat")
The AI finds patterns in those examples
It builds a mathematical model of those patterns
When it sees a new photo, it uses that model to guess: cat or not cat?
Over time, with more data and corrections, it gets better and better
Think of it like studying for an exam: the more practice questions you do, the better you get. AI does the same thing — but it can do millions of practice questions in seconds.

rows:
AI is not magic. It is maths + data + computing power.
Every AI system was built by humans and reflects human choices about what data to use.
AI is a tool — like a hammer or a calculator. What matters is how you use it.

t: Activity 2 — AI Myth Busters

rows:
Read each myth aloud. Students vote TRUE or FALSE by raising their hands or holding up a card.
Reveal the answer and give the brief explanation. Award points for correct answers.
This works best as a lively, fast-paced quiz — keep energy high!

t: MYTH 1: "AI is conscious and has feelings."

rows: AI systems process information mathematically. They do not experience emotions, consciousness, or awareness. When ChatGPT says "I feel excited about this," it is generating text that sounds human — not actually feeling anything. This is called the Chinese Room problem (a famous thought experiment by philosopher John Searle).

t: MYTH 2: "AI knows everything."

rows: AI only knows what it was trained on. If the training data stops at a certain date, the AI knows nothing after that. It can also be completely wrong (this is called a "hallucination") — it can confidently state false facts. Always verify AI answers from trusted sources.

t: MYTH 3: "AI will take all human jobs."

rows: AI will automate some tasks (repetitive, rule-based ones), but it also creates new jobs. The key insight: AI cannot replace human creativity, emotional intelligence, ethical judgment, or physical dexterity in complex situations. The future belongs to people who know how to work with AI — which is exactly what this course teaches you.

t: MYTH 4: "AI is always smarter than humans."

rows: AI is superhuman at narrow tasks with clear patterns (chess, protein folding, image recognition). It fails badly at common sense reasoning, novel situations it hasn't seen before, and anything requiring genuine understanding. A 5-year-old can understand "the cup is on the table and the table is in the kitchen, so the cup is in the kitchen" — most AI still struggles with this kind of reasoning.

t: MYTH 5: "AI makes unbiased decisions."

rows: AI learns from human-generated data, which contains human biases. Amazon's hiring AI was found to discriminate against women. Facial recognition AI is less accurate on dark-skinned faces. AI inherits our biases and can even amplify them. This is why human oversight is always essential.

DAY 2
How AI Learns
Training data, patterns, and your first machine learning experiment

t: Overview

Yesterday we discovered that AI is already everywhere. Today we go inside the machine. We will explore how AI actually learns — through data, patterns, and repetition — and then you will train your very own AI model using Google's Teachable Machine. No coding required.
By the end of today, you will understand the full loop of machine learning: collect data → train the model → test it → improve it. This loop is the foundation of almost every AI system on the planet.

rows:
Explain the machine learning training loop in plain language
Understand what training data, features, labels, and models are
Experience the "sorting exercise" to feel what training data means
Train a real image classifier using Google Teachable Machine

t: Key Concepts
1. The Machine Learning Training Loop
Every machine learning system follows the same basic cycle. Understanding this loop unlocks your ability to think about any AI system:
Collect Data: Gather thousands (or millions) of examples
Label the Data: Tell the AI what each example is (e.g., "cat" or "dog")
Train the Model: The AI finds mathematical patterns that link examples to labels
Evaluate: Test the model on new examples it has never seen
Improve: Add more data, fix mistakes, retrain — repeat until good enough
Deploy: Release the model to be used in the real world
2. What Is a Feature?
A feature is any measurable characteristic the AI uses to make decisions. For example, when recognising a cat:
Pointy ears → a feature
Fur pattern → a feature
Eye shape → a feature
Body size → a feature
The AI doesn't know these are "cat features" until it sees thousands of labelled cat photos and learns which combinations reliably predict "cat."
3. Types of Machine Learning

rows: You provide labelled examples. Most common type. Used for: image recognition, spam filters, voice assistants.
No labels — AI finds patterns on its own. Used for: customer grouping, anomaly detection.
AI learns by trial and error, rewarded for good actions. Used for: games, robotics, self-driving cars.
Take a pre-trained model and fine-tune it for a new task. Powers most modern AI apps — much faster than training from scratch.

t: 4. Overfitting and Underfitting — Why AI Fails
Two of the most common problems in machine learning:

rows: The AI memorises the training examples too perfectly, and fails on any new data. Like a student who memorises textbook answers but can't answer a slightly different question on the exam.
The AI hasn't learned enough. It performs badly even on training data. Like a student who barely studied and gets everything wrong.
Good AI generalises. It learns patterns that work on new, unseen data — not just data it has memorised. This is why testing on separate "held-out" data is critical before deploying any AI system.

Activity 1 — The Human Sorting Game

rows:
Print 40–60 animal images (mix of cats, dogs, birds, fish) on cards. Shuffle them.
Divide students into groups of 4. Each group receives a shuffled set of cards.
Round 1: Without talking, each student sorts the cards into piles — any way they like.
Round 2: Reveal the "correct labels." Students re-sort using the labels.
Discussion: What features did you use to sort? How did having labels change your approach?
Connect to ML: "What you just did manually is exactly what machine learning does automatically — but with millions of examples and millions of features simultaneously."

What features did you use to tell cats from dogs? What made it hard?
If someone gave you 10,000 of these cards to sort by yourself, would you use the same method? What would you change?
What if some of the labels on the cards were wrong? How would that affect your learning?
Imagine the AI only saw white cats and orange cats. What would happen when it met a black cat for the first time?

Challenge: Improve Your Model
If your model is making mistakes, add more training images from different angles and lighting conditions, then retrain.
Did adding more data improve accuracy? Record your findings.

DAY 3
The Art of Talking to AI
Prompt engineering — your most valuable skill in the AI age

t: Figure 3.1 — Anatomy of a powerful prompt: R-C-T-F-C framework
Overview

You now understand what AI is and how it learns. But knowing about AI is not enough — you need to know how to use it effectively. Today we focus on the most important skill for working with modern AI: prompt engineering.

A prompt is the instruction you give to an AI. The same AI system can give you brilliant, useful answers or confusing, wrong answers — entirely depending on how you ask. This session teaches you to ask like a professional.

rows:
Understand what a prompt is and why it matters
Apply the R-C-T-F-C framework for writing powerful prompts
Transform weak prompts into strong prompts through iteration
Compete in the Prompt Battle and discover the power of specificity
Understand chain-of-thought prompting and zero/few-shot techniques

t: Facilitator Schedule

rows: Day 2 recap — 3 students share their Teachable Machine findings

t: Key Concepts
1. Why Prompts Matter So Much

Think of an AI language model like a brilliant expert who knows about almost everything. But this expert is locked in a room and can only communicate through written notes. How you write your note determines everything about the quality of the response.
Research shows that rephrasing the same question can change AI output quality by 50–80%. Prompt engineering is not a trick — it is a core professional skill.
2. The R-C-T-F-C Prompt Framework
Use this five-part structure to build powerful prompts:

rows: Tell the AI who to be. "You are an expert marine biologist with 20 years of experience teaching teenagers..."
Give background. "I am a 12-year-old in Australia doing a school project on coral reefs..."
State exactly what you want. "Explain how rising ocean temperatures affect coral bleaching..."
Specify the output structure. "...in 3 short paragraphs with a summary bullet list at the end."
Add limits. "...Avoid scientific jargon. Do not include information I cannot verify online."

t: 3. Prompt Levels — From Weak to Expert
Level 1 — Weak Prompt: "Tell me about space."
Result: A vague, generic response that could fill a textbook — completely unhelpful.
Level 2 — Better Prompt: "Explain black holes to a 12-year-old."
Result: More targeted, but still quite broad.

Level 3 — Expert Prompt: "You are a science teacher for Year 7 students in Australia. Explain what a black hole is using one everyday analogy, three key facts, and a 'mind-blowing' closing statement. Keep the total under 200 words."
Result: Precise, useful, perfectly formatted response.
4. Advanced Techniques
Chain-of-Thought: Add "Think step by step" to get the AI to show its reasoning. Great for maths and logic problems.
Few-Shot Prompting: Give examples of what you want before asking. "Here are two examples of good haiku: [example 1], [example 2]. Now write one about summer."
Role Reversal: Ask the AI to ask YOU questions to clarify what it needs before answering.
Negative Constraints: Tell the AI what NOT to do. "Do not use bullet points. Do not include dates."
Iteration: Treat your first prompt as a draft. Read the response, then refine: "That's good but too long. Shorten it to 3 sentences and add one real-world example."

rows: Expert AI users never accept the first response. They iterate. They refine. They experiment. Think of prompting like a conversation, not a single question. Each follow-up narrows the AI closer to exactly what you need.

t: Activity 2 — The Prompt Battle Championship

rows:
Divide into 4 teams. Each round, all teams get the SAME task (displayed on projector).

Round 2 — CREATE: "Get the AI to write the most suspenseful opening paragraph for a novel — no more than 80 words."
Round 3 — SOLVE: "Get the AI to help you plan a birthday party for 15 people with a budget of $100 AUD."
Round 4 — PERSUADE: "Get the AI to write the most convincing argument for a 4-day school week."
Round 5 — IMAGINE: "Get the AI to describe what daily life will look like in Australia in 2050 — be specific and surprising."

Prompt: The instruction or question you give to an AI
Prompt Engineering: The skill of crafting effective prompts to get high-quality AI outputs
Context Window: The maximum amount of text an AI can "see" at once
Chain-of-Thought: Prompting technique that asks AI to show its reasoning step by step
Few-Shot Prompting: Providing examples in your prompt to guide the AI's response style
Iteration: Refining a prompt based on the AI's response to get a better result
Constraint: A rule you give the AI about what NOT to do or include
Temperature: An AI setting that controls how creative/random vs predictable its outputs are

DAY 4
AI Eyes, Ears & More
Computer vision, speech AI, and the art of spotting what's real

t: Overview

Today we explore how AI perceives the world through cameras and microphones. Computer vision and speech recognition are two of the most widely deployed AI technologies on Earth — from the camera on your phone to Siri to medical imaging. We will also investigate one of the most important challenges of our time: telling AI-generated content from real content.

rows:
Understand how computer vision works (feature detection, CNNs)
Understand how speech recognition converts audio to text
Explore real-world applications: medical imaging, self-driving cars, translation
Develop critical skills for detecting AI-generated images, text, and audio
Discuss the ethical implications of deepfakes and synthetic media

rows: Day 3 recap — prompt battle winners share winning prompts

t: Key Concepts
1. How Computer Vision Works
Computer vision teaches machines to "see" and interpret images and video. Here is the process:
Input: An image enters the system as a grid of pixels — each pixel is just a number representing colour
Convolution: Filters scan across the image looking for low-level features: edges, corners, curves, colours
Pooling: The AI compresses information, keeping the most important features while reducing complexity
Deep layers: Higher layers combine simple features into complex ones: edge + curve = eye, multiple eyes + nose = face
Classification: The final layer outputs a prediction: "This is a cat with 94% confidence"
This architecture is called a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). It is the backbone of nearly all modern image AI.
2. Computer Vision in the Real World

rows: AI reads X-rays, MRI scans, and retinal images to detect cancer, diabetic blindness, and bone fractures — often matching or exceeding radiologist accuracy
Multiple cameras + radar detect roads, signs, pedestrians, and obstacles in real time at 30+ frames per second
Your phone maps 30,000 infrared dots onto your face to create a mathematical model — impossible to fake with a photo
Real-time face landmark detection (68+ points) that tracks your face movement and applies effects precisely
Factories use AI cameras to detect defects in products at speeds impossible for human inspectors
Point your camera at text, objects, plants, or food and AI identifies them instantly

t: 3. How Speech Recognition Works
When you speak to Siri or Google Assistant, here is what happens in milliseconds:
Audio capture: Your voice is captured as a waveform — a series of numbers representing sound pressure over time
Feature extraction: The AI analyses frequency patterns, called Mel-frequency cepstral coefficients (MFCCs) — essentially a mathematical fingerprint of each sound
Phoneme recognition: The AI maps audio patterns to phonemes (the basic units of sound in language, e.g. "sh", "ee", "p")
Language model: A language model converts phonemes into words, using context to choose between similar-sounding options ("bare" vs "bear")
Intent recognition: A final layer understands what you actually want and triggers the correct action
4. Generative AI & Synthetic Media
The same techniques that help AI recognise faces can be reversed to generate new faces. This is done with Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs):
Generator: Creates fake images, trying to fool the discriminator
Discriminator: Tries to tell real from fake
They compete against each other — as the discriminator gets better, the generator improves, until the fakes are indistinguishable from real.

rows: Deepfake technology can now create realistic videos of real people saying things they never said. AI can clone a voice from 3 seconds of audio. AI can generate photorealistic images of people who do not exist. This makes media literacy — the ability to critically evaluate what you see and hear — one of the most important skills of your generation.

Activity 1 — Spot the AI

rows:
Before class: collect 20 items — a mix of real photos (from news sites), AI-generated images (from Midjourney/DALL-E), real audio clips, and AI-generated voices.
Present each item one at a time. Students write REAL or AI on a response card.
After all 20, reveal answers and discuss the clues that gave each one away.

🔍  Student Detective Guide — How to Spot AI-Generated Images

Check These Areas First:
HANDS: AI consistently struggles with fingers — look for extra fingers, merged fingers, or hands that look "melted"
TEETH: AI-generated smiles often have too many teeth, oddly sized teeth, or teeth that blend together
BACKGROUNDS: Background objects often repeat, blur unnaturally, or contain garbled text and impossible architecture
TEXT: Any text in an AI image (signs, labels, newspapers) is almost always garbled nonsense
EARS & HAIR: Ears are often asymmetrical; hair may flow through objects or disappear
LIGHTING: Light sources may be inconsistent — shadows pointing in impossible directions
Tools for Verification:
Google Reverse Image Search — right click any image → "Search image with Google"
AI or Not (aiornot.com) — paste any image URL for instant AI detection
Hive Moderation (hivemoderation.com/deepfake-detection) — free deepfake and AI image detector

DAY 5
When AI Gets It Wrong
Bias, hallucinations, and the ethics of intelligent machines

t: Overview

Today is possibly the most important day of Week 1. Understanding AI's capabilities is only half the picture — understanding its failures, biases, and ethical challenges is what makes you a truly responsible and powerful AI user. We will run a mock AI Ethics Court, examine real-world cases of AI failure, and develop your personal framework for thinking about "should we?" not just "can we?"

rows:
Understand what AI hallucination is and why it happens
Identify the main sources and types of AI bias
Analyse real-world cases where AI caused harm
Apply an ethical reasoning framework to AI decision-making scenarios
Articulate your own position on 2–3 AI ethics debates

t: Figure 5.1 — Five sources of AI bias
Facilitator Schedule

rows: Week 1 knowledge check quiz (Days 1–4 vocabulary and concepts)

t: Key Concepts
1. AI Hallucination — When AI Makes Things Up
AI language models can confidently state things that are completely false. This is called hallucination — a technical term, not a metaphor. It happens because:
AI language models predict the most statistically likely next word, not the most true next word
They have no internal sense of "I don't know" — they fill gaps with plausible-sounding text
Their training data contained errors and biases which are reproduced in outputs
Real examples of damaging hallucinations:
Legal: Lawyers submitted court briefs citing cases that ChatGPT invented — the cases did not exist. The lawyers faced disciplinary action.
Medical: AI chatbots have given incorrect drug dosage information to patients.
Academic: Students cited AI-generated references that were entirely fabricated, including fake DOI numbers.

rows: ⚠️  The Golden Rule on AI Outputs

Never present AI-generated facts without verification from a trusted primary source. AI is a starting point for research, not the ending point. If you cannot find the fact independently confirmed, do not use it.

t: 2. Bias in AI Systems
AI bias occurs when a system produces systematically unfair or inaccurate results for certain groups. There are several ways bias enters AI:

rows: Training data reflects historical human discrimination. E.g., hiring AI trained on past employees (mostly male engineers) learned to prefer male candidates.
Certain groups are underrepresented in training data. E.g., facial recognition trained mostly on light-skinned faces performs worse on dark-skinned faces.
The data collected doesn't accurately represent reality. E.g., using arrests as a proxy for crime — biased policing skews the data.
One model applied across different groups where differences matter. E.g., a medical AI trained on average data may perform poorly for women if symptoms present differently.

t: 3. Real-World AI Failures — Case Studies
Case 1: COMPAS Recidivism Algorithm (USA, 2016)

An AI system used by US courts to predict whether criminals would reoffend was found to be twice as likely to incorrectly flag Black defendants as high-risk compared to white defendants, even when other factors were identical. The algorithm was used to influence sentencing decisions.
Case 2: Amazon Hiring AI (2018)

Amazon built an AI to screen job applications. It was trained on 10 years of resumes — which mostly came from men, as tech is male-dominated. The AI learned to penalise resumes containing the word "women's" (e.g., "women's chess club president"). Amazon scrapped the system.
Case 3: Uber/Lyft Surge Pricing in Disasters

During natural disasters, algorithmic pricing systems have charged vulnerable people thousands of dollars for emergency transport — the algorithm optimising for revenue with no ethical override for crisis situations.
Case 4: AI Health Chatbots Giving Dangerous Advice

Multiple cases have emerged of AI wellness chatbots encouraging self-harm when users expressed distress, or providing incorrect medication information. These systems lacked appropriate safety guardrails.
4. An Ethical Framework for AI Decisions
When evaluating whether an AI system should be built or deployed, ask these questions:
Fairness: Does this AI treat all groups equitably? Who might be disadvantaged?
Transparency: Can we explain why the AI made a specific decision? Is it a "black box"?
Accountability: Who is responsible when the AI makes a harmful error — the developer, the deployer, or the user?
Privacy: What data does this AI collect? Who has access to it? Can it be misused?
Safety: What happens if this AI fails? Have edge cases been tested? Is there a human override?
Consent: Do the people affected by this AI know about it? Did they agree to be affected by it?

Activity — AI Ethics Court

rows:
Divide the class into 3 groups. Each group is assigned one Case below.
Each group has 20 minutes to prepare their case — they must argue BOTH sides (prosecution and defence).
Then the class votes: Should this AI system be allowed? Yes / No / Only with conditions?
The facilitator plays "Chief Justice" — asking probing questions of each side.

The Scenario:
A government wants to use an AI system to assist judges in deciding whether to grant bail to accused criminals. The AI analyses 200 data points and predicts re-offending risk. Supporters say it removes human prejudice. Critics say it encodes historical bias and removes human judgment from life-changing decisions.
Arguments to prepare:
FOR: Consistency, speed, removes individual judge bias, evidence-based
AGAINST: Bias in historical data, no transparency, people deserve human judgment, no appeal process
Your verdict: Should this AI be used? If yes — what conditions must apply?

The Scenario:
A company releases an AI chatbot designed to provide mental health support to teenagers who cannot access professional therapy. The AI has conversations, provides coping strategies, and tracks mood. 2 million teens use it. One case emerges where the AI failed to detect serious warning signs and a teen was harmed.
Arguments to prepare:
FOR: Fills a massive gap in mental health services, available 24/7, no stigma, many helped
AGAINST: Not a licensed therapist, cannot handle crisis, single failure = life consequences, privacy of intimate disclosures
Your verdict: Should this AI remain available? Under what rules?
⚖️  Case 3: AI School Exam Cheating Detection

The Scenario:
A school adopts an AI proctoring system that monitors students during online exams via their webcam — tracking eye movement, keystrokes, facial expressions, and background audio to detect cheating. Studies show the system flags students of colour and those with learning disabilities at higher rates.
Arguments to prepare:
FOR: Academic integrity, equal standards, scales to online learning, deters cheating
AGAINST: Discriminatory outcomes, privacy invasion of home, anxiety impact, home environment advantage
Your verdict: Should this AI be used in schools? What regulations should govern it?

Hallucination: AI outputting confident but factually incorrect information
Bias: Systematic unfairness in AI outputs, usually from skewed training data
Black Box: An AI system whose internal decision-making process cannot be explained
Explainability / XAI: The ability to interpret and explain how an AI reached a conclusion

DAY 6
AI + Creativity
When machines make art — and how humans make it better

t: Overview

Welcome to Week 2. You now have the foundation to start creating. Today we explore AI as a creative partner — not a replacement for human creativity, but an amplifier of it. We will make music, write poetry, generate visual art, and ask the big question: is AI-generated art still "art"?

Use AI tools to create original creative works

t: Key Concepts
1. How Generative AI Creates

Generative AI is trained to create new content rather than just classify or predict. The key technique is called a Diffusion Model (for images) or a Large Language Model / Transformer (for text and music):

For images (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, Midjourney): Start with pure noise → the AI gradually de-noises the image, guided by your text prompt, until it produces a coherent picture that matches your description
For text (GPT-4, Claude): Predict the most likely next token (word fragment) given all previous tokens + training — do this thousands of times to produce a paragraph

For music (Suno, Udio, MusicGen): Similar to image generation — the AI has learned the patterns of pitch, rhythm, timbre, and structure from millions of songs, and generates new audio that follows these patterns
2. The Creativity Debate

rows: AI has never had an original thought. It recombines patterns from human-created works. True creativity requires consciousness, lived experience, and emotional intent.
"AI creativity is real — the output is new."
If the result is genuinely novel and valuable, does the process matter? Humans also build on existing knowledge and culture. The output is what counts.
The most creative work happens when humans use AI to explore possibilities they couldn't reach alone, then apply judgment, emotion, and intention to shape the result.

t: 3. Copyright and Ownership
AI-generated content raises complex legal questions that are actively being debated in courts worldwide:
In Australia, copyright currently requires a human author — AI-only works may not be protected
If you use AI as a tool and make substantial creative decisions, you may hold copyright over the final work
Training AI on copyrighted artworks without permission is being challenged in multiple lawsuits globally
Platform terms vary widely — always check whether you own what you create on any AI platform

t: Key Concepts
1. Design Thinking — The 5 Stages

rows: Understand the people who have the problem. Do not assume — ask, observe, listen. Who is affected? How does this problem feel to them?
Clearly state the problem. Use this format: "[Person] needs [need] because [insight]." Avoid jumping to solutions.
Generate as many ideas as possible without judging any of them. Quantity over quality at this stage. Wild ideas welcome.
Build the simplest possible version of your solution — fast and cheap. A sketch, a flowchart, a simple demo is fine.
Get real feedback. Show your prototype to someone who has the problem. What works? What doesn't? Iterate.

t: 2. Where Does AI Actually Help?
Not every problem needs AI. Here is a framework for deciding when AI adds value:

rows: ✅  AI is a good fit when...

The task involves pattern recognition in large amounts of data (e.g., identifying disease in thousands of images)
Personalisation at scale is needed (e.g., recommending different content to millions of users)
Speed matters more than occasional errors (e.g., spam filtering — some false positives are acceptable)
The task is repetitive and rule-based at its core, but with too many variations for manual rules
Natural language understanding or generation is central to the problem
⚠️  AI is NOT a good fit when...

The decision has life-or-death consequences and errors are unacceptable without human review
You have very little training data (AI needs lots of examples to work well)
The problem requires genuine common sense, novel reasoning, or emotional nuance
Transparency and explainability are legally or ethically required (e.g., credit decisions, medical diagnosis)
A simple rule-based system or spreadsheet would do the job just as well

t: 3. Project Options for Days 8–9
Choose one of the following project tracks. Each is achievable in approximately 2 × 3 hours with the provided tools:

rows: Build a custom AI assistant for a specific topic using a system prompt. Example: a "homework helper" that only assists with science questions, always shows working, and never just gives the answer.
Build an image classifier using Teachable Machine and embed it in a simple webpage. Example: a "plant health checker" that identifies healthy vs diseased leaves.
Build an interactive choose-your-own-adventure story powered by AI. The reader's choices update the AI prompt, generating new story branches.
Design a prompt system that helps students research a topic responsibly — breaking down a topic, suggesting sources, summarising findings, and flagging potential misinformation.

t: Activity 1 — Problem Sprint

rows:
Give each student 10 sticky notes. Set a 5-minute timer.
Task: Write one problem per sticky note — problems from school, home, your community, your interests. No filtering.
Group students into teams of 3. Each team clusters their problems by theme on a large sheet of paper.
Teams pick ONE problem to pursue. It should be: specific, genuinely felt, and potentially solvable with a digital tool.

Complete this statement to define your problem:
WHO has this problem? (Be specific — "Year 7 students who study at home alone" not just "students")
WHAT is the problem? (Describe the situation, not the solution)
WHEN/WHERE does this problem occur?
WHY does it matter? What is the impact if it remains unsolved?
HOW do people currently solve it? What is wrong with the current solution?
Your Problem Statement: "[Person] needs [need] because [insight]."
Example: "Year 7 students studying alone at home need a way to check their own understanding of maths concepts because they have no one to ask and get stuck for hours, losing motivation."

t: Overview

Today is pure building. Teams work on their chosen project track, supported by facilitators and peer collaboration. The goal by end of day: a working prototype — even a rough one — that demonstrates your core idea.
Remember the Design Thinking principle: a bad prototype you can test beats a perfect plan you can only imagine. Done is better than perfect on Day 8.

rows:
Have a working proof-of-concept by end of day
Identify the biggest technical or design challenge in your project
Test your prototype with at least one person outside your team
Document what works, what doesn't, and what you'll fix tomorrow

t: Track A — Smart Chatbot Build Guide

rows: 📋  Tools: Claude.ai or ChatGPT (free tier) + Google Docs to record outputs
A "smart chatbot" for a specific purpose is built through a carefully crafted system prompt — the instruction that defines who the AI is, what it knows, and how it should behave.
📝  System Prompt Template for Your Chatbot

Copy this template and fill in the [brackets]:
You are [name], an AI assistant specialising in [topic]. You help [target user] with [specific task].
Your personality is [3 adjectives — e.g. warm, patient, encouraging].
Rules you always follow:
Only answer questions related to [topic]. For other topics, politely redirect.
Always [key behaviour — e.g. show your working step by step].
Never [prohibited behaviour — e.g. give the answer directly without explanation].
If you are unsure, say so clearly and suggest where the user could find a reliable answer.
Always end your response with a follow-up question to check understanding.
Test your system prompt: paste it into Claude or ChatGPT as the first message, then have a classmate try to use your chatbot. Does it behave as intended?

t: Track B — Image Classifier App Build Guide

rows: 📝  Step-by-Step: Teachable Machine → Embedded Web App

Phase 1: Train Your Model (30 min)
Plan your classes: what 3–4 categories will your classifier recognise?
Collect training images: minimum 100 per class, from multiple angles, lighting, backgrounds
Train on Teachable Machine (teachablemachine.withgoogle.com)
Evaluate accuracy — aim for 85%+ before proceeding
Phase 2: Export and Embed (30 min)
Click "Export Model" → "Tensorflow.js" → "Upload my model" → copy the model URL
Open a new file called index.html on your computer
Paste in the Teachable Machine sample code from the "Code snippets" section
Replace the placeholder model URL with yours
Open index.html in your browser — it should work immediately
Phase 3: Customise (30 min)
Change the page title, add a description of what your classifier does
Add a friendly message when each class is detected
Share your index.html file with another team and let them test it

t: Track C — AI Story Generator Build Guide

rows:
Concept: The player makes choices. Each choice is fed back into the AI prompt to generate the next scene.
Phase 1: Story Architecture (20 min)
Choose a setting and premise: e.g. "You are a young scientist in 2080 who discovers an AI that has gained emotions"
Map your story tree: 3 chapters, each with 2 choices. That's 8 possible story paths.
Write the world-building context (2–3 paragraphs) that the AI will use as background
Phase 2: System Prompt Design (20 min)
Write a system prompt that sets the tone: "You are a narrative AI for an interactive science fiction story. The tone is [adjective]. Always end each scene with exactly 2 choices formatted as: CHOICE A: [option] | CHOICE B: [option]"
Test it: does the AI maintain consistency? Does it remember previous choices?
Phase 3: User Interface (40 min)
Create a simple Google Slides or PowerPoint presentation
Slide 1: Opening scene (paste AI-generated text). Add 2 buttons linking to Slide 2A or 2B.
Each branch slide: generate that scene from the AI using the choice as context
Continue for 3 chapters — you will have approximately 14 slides total

t: Track D — AI Research Assistant Build Guide

rows:
Concept: Design a series of prompts that help a student research any topic responsibly.
The 5-Prompt Research System — design each prompt:
SCOPE PROMPT: "Given the research question [X], break it into 5 specific sub-questions that together would fully answer it."
SOURCES PROMPT: "For the sub-question [Y], suggest 5 types of sources I should look for. Include at least 2 primary source types."
SUMMARISE PROMPT: "Here is text I found [paste source text]. Summarise the key points relevant to [sub-question]. Flag any claims that need verification."
BIAS CHECK PROMPT: "Analyse this summary for potential bias, missing perspectives, or unsupported claims: [paste summary]."
SYNTHESIS PROMPT: "Using these verified points [list], write a 3-paragraph evidence-based response to [original research question]. Cite each point."
Test your system: use it to research a topic you actually need for school. Does it produce better research than googling alone?

t: User Testing Protocol

rows: 📋  How to Run a Proper User Test

User testing is not asking "do you like it?" — that gets you polite answers, not useful ones.
Follow this protocol for each tester:

BEFORE: Set up
Tell the tester: "We are testing the tool, not you. There are no wrong answers. Please think out loud as you use it."
Give them a specific task: "Imagine you need to [specific use case]. Try using this tool to accomplish that."
DURING: Observe, don't help
Do NOT help them when they get stuck — their confusion is your most valuable data
Note exactly where they pause, hesitate, or make mistakes
Write down their exact words when they react to anything
AFTER: Ask these 5 questions
What would you use this for if you had it right now?
What confused you most?
What was the most useful part?
What one thing would you change?
Would you recommend this to a friend? Why or why not?

t: Demo Day Presentation Template

rows:
Section 1: The Problem — 45 seconds
Open with a real scenario: "Imagine you are [user] and you face [problem]..."
Make the audience feel the problem before you show the solution
Section 2: Our Solution — 60 seconds
Show (not just tell) — live demo or screenshot walkthrough
Explain what AI does vs what the human does
Highlight the most impressive/surprising thing it can do
Section 3: What We Learned — 45 seconds
What was the hardest thing to build?
What did the AI do that surprised you?
What would you improve with more time?
Section 4: The Bigger Picture — 30 seconds
If this tool existed fully developed, what impact could it have?
Close with your team's key insight from this project

DAY 10
Demo Day & Graduation
Two weeks. Ten days. One showcase. Your AI future begins now.

t: Overview

This is the day everything comes together. You will present your projects to a real audience — parents, teachers, or guests — and demonstrate not just what you built, but how you think. This session is also a celebration of how far you have come in two weeks.

rows:
Deliver your 3-minute project presentation with confidence

Complete your "Letter to Future Self" — a personal commitment

t: The Demo Day Mindset

rows:
Start with the problem, not the technology. The audience needs to care before they can be impressed.
Show the tool working live — even if it's imperfect. A live demo with one bug is more compelling than a flawless recording.
Speak to what you learned, not just what you built. Judges value insight over polish.
If something goes wrong during the demo, say so calmly and explain what it should do. Handling failure gracefully is itself impressive.
Thank your team, your facilitator, and anyone who gave you feedback during user testing.

t: Audience Questions — Facilitator Guide

rows: 📋  Suggested Questions for Audience Members and Guests

What made you choose this particular problem to solve?
How did you decide which parts of the solution to give to AI and which to keep for humans?
What would happen if someone tried to misuse your tool?
If you had 3 more weeks, what would you build next?
What did AI do that surprised you during this project?
What did you discover that you wish you'd known before you started building?

t: Award Categories

rows: 🏆  Audience Choice Awards (all teams eligible for all categories)

Most Innovative Solution — tackled a problem in an unexpected way
Best User Experience — easiest and most enjoyable to use
Greatest Real-World Impact — could help the most people if fully built
Best Use of AI — most thoughtful and effective use of AI capabilities
Most Ethical Design — best handling of fairness, privacy, and safety concerns
Best Presentation — clearest storytelling and most engaging delivery

t: Letter to My Future Self

rows:
Provide each student with a piece of quality paper and an envelope.
Allow 15 minutes of quiet, reflective writing. Play soft instrumental music.
Students seal and address the envelope to themselves. Facilitators mail them in 6 months.
Tell students: "This letter will arrive when you are in the middle of a new school year. It is a message from the version of you who just finished this program."
📝  Letter to My Future Self — Prompts

Date: ___
Dear Future Me,
Right now, here is what I understand about AI that I didn't two weeks ago:
The project I built was called [name]. Here is what I'm most proud of about it:
The moment that surprised me most during this program was:
The ethical question about AI that I still think about is:
By the time you read this letter, I want to have done these things with AI:
The one thing I want you to remember about AI is:
Signed, [your name], age 12

t: Appendix — Master Vocabulary Reference
This glossary covers every key term introduced across the 10-day program. Use it as a study reference and quick-look guide.
A – D

rows: A set of step-by-step instructions for solving a problem. The foundation of all software, including AI.
Computer systems that perform tasks normally requiring human intelligence — recognising patterns, understanding language, making decisions.
Systematic unfairness in AI outputs, typically caused by skewed training data that over- or under-represents certain groups.
An AI system whose internal decision-making process cannot be inspected or explained.
A program that simulates conversation with humans, increasingly powered by large language models.
The standard neural network architecture for processing images — uses layers of filters to detect features at increasing levels of complexity.
AI that interprets and understands digital images and video.
The maximum amount of text an AI language model can "see" at once when generating a response.
AI-generated synthetic media that realistically replaces a real person's likeness, voice, or words.
A subset of machine learning using multi-layered neural networks — excels at images, language, and audio.
AI architecture for generating images — starts with noise and gradually refines it into a coherent image guided by a text prompt.

t: E – L

rows: One complete pass through all training data during model training. More epochs = more learning (up to a point).
The ability to interpret and explain how an AI system reached a specific conclusion — critical for high-stakes decisions.
The principle that an AI system should treat all groups equitably and not cause disproportionate harm to any group.
A measurable characteristic that an AI uses to make decisions — e.g. "edge angle" in image recognition.
AI architecture with two competing networks — a generator that creates fake content and a discriminator that tries to detect fakes.
AI that creates new content (text, images, audio, video) rather than just classifying or predicting.
Constraints built into AI systems to prevent harmful, biased, or off-topic outputs.
When an AI language model outputs confident but factually incorrect information — a core limitation of current LLMs.
The correct answer attached to a training example — e.g. "cat" or "not cat" — used in supervised learning.
AI trained on vast amounts of text to understand and generate human language — the technology behind ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini.
The multi-dimensional mathematical space inside an AI model where concepts are encoded as numerical relationships.

t: M – R

rows: A subset of AI where systems learn from data rather than being explicitly programmed with rules.
The mathematical structure an AI builds from training data — encodes learned patterns and is used to make predictions.
AI that understands, interprets, and generates human language in text or speech form.
A computing architecture loosely inspired by the brain — layers of interconnected nodes process information in parallel.
AI that excels at one specific task but cannot transfer skills to other domains. All current AI is narrow AI.
When an AI memorises training data too closely, performing perfectly on training examples but failing on new data.
A learnable numerical value inside a neural network that is adjusted during training to minimise errors.
The instruction or question you provide to an AI system to guide its output.
The skill of crafting effective, specific instructions to get high-quality outputs from AI systems.
ML where an AI agent learns by trial and error — rewarded for correct actions, penalised for wrong ones.

t: S – Z

rows: AI that converts spoken audio into written text.
ML where the AI is trained on labelled examples — the most common type of machine learning today.
Hidden instructions given to an AI chatbot that define its persona, rules, and constraints.
A setting that controls how creative/random (high temperature) vs predictable/focused (low temperature) an AI's outputs are.
The basic unit an LLM processes — roughly a word fragment (e.g. "unbelievable" = 3 tokens: "un", "believ", "able").
The examples used to teach an AI — the quality and diversity of training data largely determines AI quality.
Adapting a pre-trained model for a new task rather than training from scratch — dramatically reduces data and compute requirements.
When an AI hasn't learned enough from training data and performs poorly even on familiar examples.
ML where the AI finds patterns in unlabelled data without being told what to look for.
A research field focused on making AI decision-making transparent and interpretable by humans.
